responses.text: The BuzzFeed News article accurately frames its central point: the 2020 National Academies report did call directed, pulsed radiofrequency energy the most plausible mechanism among the causes it considered, while also reporting strong expert criticism and emphasizing that the finding was not conclusive.

The National Academies report itself says the State Department asked for a review of reported illnesses among personnel in Havana and Guangzhou, notes that evidence was limited and no hypothesis had been proven, and describes the circumstances as unclear.

The report’s radiofrequency discussion supports BuzzFeed’s description because it says many acute early symptoms were consistent with RF effects and cites the Frey effect as a mechanism by which pulsed RF can produce perceived sounds without identifiable structural injury.

The National Academies press release adds important context that the committee could not rule out other mechanisms, thought multiple factors likely explained some cases, and faced serious limits from variable clinical cases and lack of access to specific health or personal information.

The State Department’s contemporaneous caution, quoted by NPR as well as BuzzFeed, was that “consistent with” pulsed radiofrequency energy allowed plausibility but did not assign cause, which supports BuzzFeed’s warning against reading the National Academies finding as proof of a microwave attack.

The article’s skepticism was also supported by later technical review: the 2022 JASON report said short-pulse RF could not be ruled out for a subset but was unlikely because no well-documented, broadly accepted mechanism could induce lasting neurological damage with focusable RF energy without skin heating.

The same JASON report further said no single hypothesized mechanism explained all incidents, that most reported incidents were consistent with common pathophysiological or environmental origins, and that it was not possible to conclude the events were intentional attacks causing physical harm.

A 2022 Intelligence Community experts panel partly conflicted with the most skeptical view by concluding that pulsed electromagnetic energy, especially in the radiofrequency range, plausibly explained a subset of cases with core characteristics, while also noting information gaps and not assessing attribution to any actor.

The strongest later official assessment cut against a foreign microwave-weapon explanation: the 2023 Intelligence Community assessment said most agencies judged it “very unlikely” that a foreign adversary was responsible and found no credible evidence that an adversary had a weapon or collection device causing the incidents.

The 2023 assessment also said medical and technical analysis had not borne out key assumptions from the initial Cuba cases and that symptoms were probably due to factors not involving a foreign adversary, such as preexisting conditions, conventional illnesses, environmental factors, and social factors.

Subsequent NIH research in JAMA found no significant differences between 86 AHI participants and 30 vocationally matched controls in most auditory, vestibular, cognitive, visual, or blood-biomarker tests, although AHI participants had more imbalance, fatigue, PTSD, depression symptoms, and functional neurological disorders.

Earlier medical literature did document real reported symptoms among a small Havana cohort, with a 2018 JAMA case series describing persistent cognitive, vestibular, oculomotor, sleep, and headache problems after reported directional audible or sensory phenomena of unclear origin.

The article’s mention of cricket-noise alternatives had a factual basis because a 2019 acoustic analysis found the Associated Press recording spectrally matched the echoing call of the Indies short-tailed cricket, while explicitly saying the cause of personnel health problems was beyond that paper’s scope.

Bottom line: BuzzFeed’s 2020 article is broadly trustworthy as a skeptical account of an overinterpreted National Academies conclusion, but the best current evidence leaves the underlying causes heterogeneous and unresolved rather than proving either a microwave attack or a purely psychogenic explanation.
